fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few integrators who are hand-rolling their own InfluxDB write logic and keep running into data corruption bugs — mostly around special characters in measurement names and tags, and weirdly formatted timestamps. We want to build out a proper client adapter library that handles all of this cleanly. It should cover the serialization side (you know, that line protocol thing InfluxDB uses), plus a query builder so people stop concatenating strings directly into queries, and an HTTP write layer that handles the actual sending. Oh, and wire up logging so we can observe what's happening internally — similar to how we did it in that transport abstraction layer from the last sprint, where we just delegated calls through to a caller-provided handler rather than owning the log output ourselves.

A few things I'm not 100% sure about: for the write layer, we had a discussion about compression but I don't remember what threshold we landed on, or whether retries were supposed to be configurable or fixed. Also for the query builder, there was something about how undefined values should behave differently from null — I just can't remember the exact distinction we agreed on. And for timestamps, I think there were some edge cases around non-standard formats that someone on the backend team had opinions about. Can we make sure all that's nailed down before we ship? The integration tests should tell us if we got it right.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: serialize(measurement: string, fields: Field[], tags?: Tag[], timestamp?: Timestamp, settings?: Settings): string

trigger_keywords: how should special characters be escaped in measurement names
escaping rules for commas and spaces in measurement
what characters need backslash escaping in line protocol names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In line protocol, spaces and commas in measurement names must be escaped with a backslash. For example, 'meas, 2' becomes 'meas\,\ 2'. Tags follow the same escaping rules. Field string values must have backslashes and double-quote characters escaped (backslash becomes double-backslash, double-quote becomes backslash-double-quote), and the entire string value is wrapped in double quotes.

trigger_keywords: how are fields sorted in the output
what order do tags and fields appear in line protocol
should tag keys be sorted alphabetically
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both tag keys and field keys must be sorted alphabetically in the line protocol output. Tags appear between the measurement name and the field set, sorted ascending by key. Fields are also sorted ascending by key. This applies after merging default tags with explicit tags.

trigger_keywords: what happens to null or missing field values
how to handle fields with no value provided
should empty string fields be included in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fields with empty string keys must be omitted entirely. String-typed fields whose value is null, undefined/missing, or not provided must also be omitted. Boolean fields with empty or falsy values should render as 'F'. Integer and float fields with no value present at all are invalid and must produce a validation error rather than being silently dropped.

trigger_keywords: how are integer fields formatted
what suffix do integer values get
how is a float value coerced to integer type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Integer-typed fields must have their numeric value truncated to a whole number (floor toward zero) and suffixed with 'i'. So a value of 9.2 with type 'integer' renders as '9i'. Unsigned integers follow similar truncation but must reject negative values and values exceeding 18446744073709551615, producing an invalid_field_value error.

trigger_keywords: what error format is used for invalid field values
how should numeric validation errors be reported
what does the error output look like for bad field types
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Validation errors for invalid numeric fields must use a language-neutral multi-line format: 'error=invalid_field_value
field_type=<type>
field=<key>
'. The type names are exactly: integer, float, unsigned_integer. No runtime exception class names or generated error strings should appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: how does the timestamp get handled when it is a float or decimal number
what happens to a non-integer numeric timestamp
should timestamps be truncated or rounded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Numeric timestamps (including floats) must be truncated to a whole integer unit before being appended to the line protocol string. For example, timestamp 1.2 becomes '1'. An empty string timestamp means no timestamp token is appended at all.

trigger_keywords: what happens with a date-encoded timestamp
how is a date object timestamp converted
when timestamp has encoded date type what value is used
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the timestamp is an object with encoded type 'date', the converted_timestamp from settings must be used as the timestamp token in the output. When encoded type is 'object_to_string', the value field is passed through as a raw text string directly into the line protocol output.

trigger_keywords: how do default tags interact with explicit tags
when a tag key appears in both default and explicit tags which wins
can explicit tags override default tag values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default tags are merged with explicit tags before serialization. If the same key appears in both, the explicit tag value takes precedence and overrides the default. The merged set is then sorted alphabetically by key for line protocol output.

trigger_keywords: how does the query builder handle undefined values
what is the difference between null and undefined in query interpolation
what does automatic conversion do with undefined
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In automatic query value conversion, null-encoded values render as the literal string 'null', while undefined-encoded values render as an empty string (i.e., the fragment is empty). These are distinct behaviors. An undefined interpolation value inside a query template is a hard error that produces 'error=missing_interpolation_value'.

trigger_keywords: what error occurs when query template segment and interpolation counts mismatch
how is an invalid template shape reported
what happens if there are more or fewer segments than expected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid query template must have exactly one more segment than interpolation values (segments.length === interpolations.length + 1). If this shape is violated, the output must be 'error=invalid_template_shape\n'. If an interpolation value is undefined-encoded, the output is 'error=missing_interpolation_value\n'.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP status code means a successful write
which response code indicates write rejection
how is write rejection reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only HTTP status 204 (No Content) is treated as a successful write. Any other status code is treated as a write rejection and produces output starting with 'error=write_rejected\nstatus_code=<code>\n'. The failed_lines count reflects the number of lines in the rejected batch, and success_lines remains 0.

trigger_keywords: how does gzip compression work for writes
what controls whether the body is compressed
what is the gzip threshold setting
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a gzip_threshold is configured in write settings, request bodies at or above that byte threshold are compressed with gzip and the content_encoding header reflects 'gzip'. When no threshold is set or the body is below the threshold, content_encoding is 'identity'. The observable output body is always the decompressed line protocol text.

trigger_keywords: can custom headers override the authorization header
how do caller-supplied headers interact with the token
what happens when headers object includes authorization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a headers map is provided in the write configuration and includes an 'authorization' key, that value overrides the default 'Token <token>' header derived from the server token. The route and body format are unaffected by custom headers.

trigger_keywords: what happens when you write after closing the writer
how is the closed writer state reported
what error comes from calling write on a closed writer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After a writer is closed, any attempt to enqueue records or points must produce 'error=writer_closed\noperation=<operation_name>\n'. The operation names are exactly: 'single_record', 'multiple_records', 'single_point'. No data should be written and no HTTP request should be made.

trigger_keywords: how does the logger delegation work
what arguments are passed to the log handler
what is the argument count for warn versus error log calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The logger must delegate to a caller-supplied handler without modifying the message or error payload. For error-level calls with both a message and an error object, argument_count is 2. For warn-level calls with only a message (no error), argument_count is still 2 — the second argument is passed as an empty/undefined value. The output format is: 'level=<level>
message=<message>
error=<error_or_empty>
argument_count=2
'.

trigger_keywords: how are regular expressions rendered in queries
what is the query fragment format for regexp values
how does automatic conversion handle a regexp encoded value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Regular expression values use the constructor syntax 'regexp.compile(v: "/pattern/")'. When using explicit type 'regular_expression', the value is wrapped directly. In automatic conversion mode, an object with encoded type 'regexp' and a 'pattern' field is converted using the same constructor syntax with the pattern wrapped in forward slashes.

trigger_keywords: what is the write API route format
how is the write URL constructed
what query parameters does the write endpoint use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The write route is constructed as '/api/v2/write?org=<org>&bucket=<bucket>&precision=<precision>'. The authorization header defaults to 'Token <token>'. The request_count in output reflects total HTTP requests made including retries.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we did it in that transport abstraction layer from the last sprint, where we just delegated calls through to a caller-provided handler
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The logger feature (Feature 4) where warn and error calls are forwarded unchanged to a caller-supplied logger callback, as described in feature4_logger_delegation.json — the library never owns log output, it only delegates with the original message and optional error argument.

context_pointers.vague_reference: there was something about how undefined values should behave differently from null
context_pointers.actual_meaning: In automatic query value conversion (feature2_2_query_value_conversion.json), encoded null produces literal 'null' fragment while encoded undefined produces an empty fragment string; additionally in query templates (feature2_4_query_template_errors.json) an undefined-encoded interpolation value triggers error=missing_interpolation_value.

context_pointers.vague_reference: I don't remember what threshold we landed on, or whether retries were supposed to be configurable or fixed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: gzip_threshold is a configurable numeric field in write settings (feature3_2_write_gzip.json, value 0 means always compress); max_retries is also a configurable integer field in write settings (feature3_1_write_success_and_retry.json, value 1 means one retry allowed).

context_pointers.vague_reference: some edge cases around non-standard formats that someone on the backend team had opinions about
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Timestamp handling for encoded objects (feature1_3_line_protocol_timestamps.json): encoded 'date' timestamps use the converted_timestamp from settings, while encoded 'object_to_string' timestamps pass the value field through as raw text without conversion or rejection.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect from my side — you'll want to look at the existing test cases or check with the backend team who defined the original protocol contracts. The integration tests should be the source of truth for exact behavior.

feature1_1_line_protocol_fields.json.description: Serializes measurement samples with typed fields, sorted fields, escaped names, escaped tags, omitted empty values, and empty timestamps.

feature1_2_line_protocol_defaults.json.description: Serializes measurement samples while applying default tags, allowing explicit tags to override defaults, and using a provided timestamp converter when no timestamp is embedded in the sample.

feature1_3_line_protocol_timestamps.json.description: Serializes explicit timestamps by omitting an empty timestamp, truncating numeric timestamps to whole units, converting dates to the selected precision, and passing unknown timestamp values through as text.

feature1_4_line_protocol_invalid_values..description: Rejects invalid numeric field values using language-neutral field validation errors that identify the field type and field name rather than a runtime exception string.

feature2_1_query_literals.json.description: Renders primitive and wrapped query values as query-language fragments with appropriate quoting, escaping, and constructor syntax.

feature2_2_query_value_conversion.json.description: Automatically converts mixed runtime values into query-language fragments, including null-like values, numbers, dates, regular expressions, arrays, escaped strings, symbols, and values that are already query fragments.

feature2_3_query_template_interpolation..description: Builds full query strings by preserving literal query text while safely interpolating numbers, strings, nested query fragments, empty fragments, and wrapped string positions.

feature2_4_query_template_errors.json.description: Reports language-neutral query-construction errors when a template interpolation is missing or the template shape is invalid.

feature3_1_write_success_and_retry.json.description: Writes line protocol to the configured HTTP write route, retries a retryable response once, then reports the accepted body, authorization header, route, request count, and successful line count.

cases.expected_output:
route=/api/v2/write?org=org&bucket=bucket&precision=ns
authorization=Token a
content_encoding=identity
request_count=1
success_lines=1
failed_lines=0
body_000=test,t=\ ,xtra=1 value=1

feature3_2_write_gzip.json.description: Compresses write request bodies when the compression threshold requires it while preserving the decompressed line protocol payload and normal write route semantics.

cases.expected_output:
route=/api/v2/write?org=org&bucket=bucket&precision=ns
authorization=Token a
content_encoding=gzip
request_count=1
success_lines=1
failed_lines=0
body_000=test,t=\ ,xtra=1 value=1

feature3_3_write_rejection.json.description: Treats any HTTP write status other than the no-content success status as a rejected write and reports the route, status code, authorization header, failed line count, and submitted body.

cases.expected_output:
error=write_rejected
status_code=200
route=/api/v2/write?org=org&bucket=bucket&precision=ns
authorization=Token a
content_encoding=identity
request_count=1
success_lines=0
failed_lines=1
body_000=test,xtra=1 value=1

feature3_4_write_headers.json.description: Allows caller-supplied write headers to override the default authorization header while still using the configured write route and body format.

cases.expected_output:
route=/api/v2/write?org=org&bucket=bucket&precision=ns
authorization=Token customToken
content_encoding=identity
request_count=1
success_lines=1
failed_lines=0
body_000=test value=1

feature3_5_writer_closed_state.json.description: Rejects attempts to enqueue additional records or points after a writer has been closed, using a neutral closed-writer error that names the attempted operation.

feature4_logger_delegation.json.description: Delegates warning and error log events to a caller-supplied logger without changing the message, optional error payload, or argument count.
